howItWorks.intro: This boat bottom paint calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.

rules.name: Wetted Surface
rules.explanation: Approximation of hull area below waterline.

howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: How much bottom paint do I need?
faq.answer: Calculate wetted surface area, then divide by coverage rate (about 400 sq ft/gal).

faq.question: How often to repaint?
faq.answer: Most antifouling paint lasts 1-2 seasons depending on water conditions.

faq.question: Is the Boat Bottom Paint Coverage Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard boating and marine formulas trusted by boaters and sailors. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What length overall should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate length overall value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Boat Bottom Paint Coverage Calculator?
sections.body: The Boat Bottom Paint Coverage Calculator is a free online tool designed for boaters and sailors who need quick, accurate calculations in the boating and marine space. By entering your length overall, beam, draft, you get instant results including wetted surface area, paint needed, estimated cost. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting wetted surface area right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In boating and marine, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by boaters and sailors worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to navigate safely with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your length overall and need to find the right wetted surface area. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Length Overall (ft)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your length overall — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: beam, draft, number of coats. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your wetted surface area, paint needed, estimated cost. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your length overall before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both wetted surface area and paint needed — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to navigate safely.
If you're unsure about your number of coats, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
